key_insight: Atmospheric plasma-sprayed nickel-graphite and copper-graphite coatings can make titanium PEMFC bipolar plates both more corrosion-resistant and more electrically conductive, with higher graphite content improving performance.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical coating route to reduce interfacial contact resistance while preserving durability in metallic bipolar plates for proton exchange membrane fuel cells.
method: Atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) used to deposit Ni-graphite and Cu-graphite coatings on titanium bipolar plates.
Graphite content was varied to study effects on phase composition, microstructure, corrosion resistance, and electrical conductivity.
Performance was evaluated using potentiostatic corrosion testing and interfacial contact resistance (ICR) measurements.
evidence_signal: The coated plates achieved low corrosion current densities (1.05 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 for Ni-G and 0.91 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 for Cu-G) and low ICR values, with Cu-G reaching 17.4 mΩ·cm^-2 before cathodic polarization.
watch_item: APS coating performance may depend strongly on graphite fraction and coating uniformity, so scale-up should verify adhesion, long-term durability, and consistency under PEMFC operating conditions.
paper_title: Investigation of Metal-Graphite Coatings for PEMFC Bipolar Plate Using Atmospheric Plasma Thermal Spraying
